attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-week history of blood in his wee. He reports no pain, frequency, or urgency. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He is a current smoker with a 40 pack-year history. He worked as a painter for 30 years before retiring.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination reveals no masses or tenderness. Digital rectal examination reveals a smooth, firm prostate.

Urinalysis confirms frank haematuria. His haemoglobin is 138 g/L (130-170). His urea, creatinine and electrolytes are within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with painless visible haematuria and significant risk factors for bladder cancer. The most likely diagnosis is transitional cell carcinoma of the bladder, given his age, smoking history, and occupational exposure to chemicals as a painter. Smoking is the strongest risk factor for bladder cancer, with smokers having a 2-4 times increased risk compared to non-smokers.

Benign prostatic hyperplasia typically presents with lower urinary tract symptoms such as frequency, urgency, and weak stream. While it can cause haematuria, this would be unusual as an isolated presenting symptom, particularly with a normal prostate examination.

Urinary tract infection typically presents with dysuria, frequency, and urgency. The absence of these symptoms, particularly in a bloke, makes this diagnosis unlikely.

Renal cell carcinoma can present with haematuria but is less common than transitional cell carcinoma. The absence of a palpable mass or systemic symptoms makes this less likely.

Urolithiasis typically presents with severe colicky pain and haematuria. The absence of pain in this case makes stone disease an unlikely cause of this patient's presentation.

The investigation of visible haematuria in adults over 45 years requires both cystoscopy and upper tract imaging (CT urogram) to evaluate for urological malignancy, regardless of whether risk factors are present. The absence of urinary symptoms does not exclude serious pathology.
learningPoints: Visible haematuria in adults over 45 years requires urgent investigation for bladder cancer.
Smoking is the strongest risk factor for bladder cancer.
Occupational exposure to chemicals (e.g., painters, dye workers) increases bladder cancer risk.
references: Dyrskjøt L, Hansel DE, Efstathiou JA, Knowles MA, Galsky MD, Teoh J, et al. Bladder cancer. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2023 Oct 26;9(1):1–21.
